04013.name: Maricopa County
04013.title: Strict Water Use Requirements for Data Centers
04013.description: Arizona's Department of Water Resources requires 100-year assured water supply plans. Maricopa County data centers face increasingly strict water use requirements amid growing scarcity. Several data center projects have been delayed or redesigned.

AZ Senate Bill 1117 (2022) — Water Use Planning

04019.name: Pima County
04019.title: Water Use Requirements for Large Facilities

sources: ADWR regulations; Tucson Water policies

04021.name: Pinal County
04021.title: Water Use Restrictions
04021.description: Pinal County lost its groundwater supply designation in 2022, directly affecting planned data center projects that rely on evaporative cooling water. Multiple data center expansions have been impacted.

06037.title: CEQA Review, Emissions Standards, and AI Procurement Rules
06037.description: LA County requires extensive CEQA environmental review for new data centers. LA's Green New Deal targets zero-emission buildings. The City of LA has implemented data center energy efficiency standards. LA County has AI procurement review requirements for government systems.

Los Angeles County — Sustainable Communities Strategy

06075.name: San Francisco County
06075.title: CEQA Review, Building Emissions Rules, and AI Surveillance Restrictions

06085.name: Santa Clara County
06085.title: CEQA Review and Water Use Requirements
06085.description: Santa Clara County and the City of San Jose require extensive CEQA environmental review including water and energy impact assessments for new data centers. Silicon Valley water agencies have imposed stricter water use requirements.

California Air Resources Board — Data Center Regulations

08031.name: Denver County
08031.title: Colorado AI Act + Active Data Center Moratoriums
08031.description: Colorado's AI Act (SB 24-205, signed May 2024) imposes disclosure obligations, impact assessments, and consumer rights for high-risk AI systems — one of the nation's most comprehensive AI laws. Denver and neighboring jurisdictions have also implemented temporary moratoriums on new large-scale data center approvals amid rapid load growth and grid capacity concerns.
08031.notes: Colorado AI Act effective date: Feb 1, 2026. Data center moratoriums at the local level are distinct from state AI law.

sources.label: Denver Ordinance 22-1551 — Building Benchmarking & Energy Efficiency
Colorado SB 21-272 — Greenhouse Gas Pollution Reduction Roadmap

sources: Denver metro data center zoning review requirements (2023–2024)

08059.name: Jefferson County
08059.title: Denver Metro Data Center Environmental Review
08059.description: Jefferson County (Lakewood/Golden, Denver metro) has proposed enhanced environmental review requirements for large data center developments, driven by Colorado's Artificial Intelligence Act (SB 24-205, 2024) and broader community concerns about energy use and emissions from large computing facilities in the Denver metropolitan area.
sources: Jefferson County CO data center review requirements (2024)

09003.name: Hartford County
09003.title: Connecticut AI in Employment Act
09003.description: Connecticut passed SB 1103 (2022) requiring notice when AI tools are used in employment decisions, affecting businesses throughout Hartford County and the state.

sources.label: Connecticut General Assembly — SB 2, AI Task Force (2023)

sources: Connecticut SB 1103 (2022)

13063.name: Clayton County
13063.title: Active Moratorium — Data Center Development Ban
13063.description: Clayton County (south Atlanta suburbs) enacted a moratorium on new data center developments following high-profile rejection of proposed facilities. The county board cited concerns about land use, noise, limited employment relative to acreage consumed, and energy grid impacts.
sources: Clayton County GA Board of Commissioners data center moratorium (2023)

13089.name: DeKalb County
13089.title: DeKalb County Data Center Moratorium (Extended through Mar 2027)
13089.description: DeKalb County Board of Commissioners enacted a moratorium on new data center approvals in June 2025 (Resolution 2025-0996) and has extended it multiple times. On July 7, 2026 the board extended the pause through March 30, 2027. On June 23, 2026 the board voted to deny the proposed Data Center Text Amendment, requiring new regulations to address water use, sewer capacity, public health, transportation, and environmental impacts before any new data center applications are accepted. Community opposition is centered on land use conflicts, limited local employment, noise from cooling equipment in residential areas, and strain on water infrastructure.
13089.notes: Moratorium extended July 7, 2026 through March 30, 2027. Data Center Text Amendment denied June 23, 2026. County working on permanent regulations.

sources.label: DeKalb County GA — Data Center Moratorium Extension Resolution 2025-1694 (official PDF)
Engage DeKalb — Data Center Text Amendment
WABE: DeKalb County Commission extends data center moratorium
Decaturish: DeKalb extends data center moratorium through early 2027

13097.name: Douglas County
13097.title: Data Center Zoning Restrictions
13097.description: Douglas County commissioners voted against approving new data center developments citing concerns about land use, limited employment creation, and community character.
sources: Douglas County Board of Commissioners, 2023

13121.name: Fulton County
13121.title: Georgia Data Center Tax Exemptions — Atlanta Metro Hub
13121.description: Fulton County (Atlanta) is a major US data center market and the core of Georgia's rapidly growing data center industry. Georgia's sales and use tax exemption on data center equipment (O.C.G.A. §48-8-3(82)) and the Atlanta area's fiber connectivity, power infrastructure, and business climate have attracted major hyperscale operators including Microsoft, Google, and multiple colocation providers.

sources.label: Georgia Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Exemptions

13135.name: Gwinnett County
13135.title: Georgia Data Center Tax Exemptions — Atlanta North Corridor Hub
13135.description: Gwinnett County (Suwanee, Lawrenceville, Duluth) is one of Metro Atlanta's fastest-growing data center submarkets. QTS Realty Trust, CyrusOne, and other major operators have significant facilities in the county. Georgia's data center sales tax exemptions and excellent fiber and power infrastructure underpin Gwinnett's growth.

sources: QTS Suwanee, GA data center; CyrusOne Atlanta campus

13217.name: Newton County
13217.title: Georgia Data Center Tax Exemptions — Google Covington Campus
13217.description: Newton County (Covington) hosts one of Google's major US data center campuses, opened in 2007 and continuously expanded. Georgia's sales and use tax exemptions on qualifying data center equipment and Georgia Power's competitive rates have made Newton County a significant data center location.

13285.name: Troup County

13285.description: Troup County (LaGrange) enacted restrictions on new large-scale data center approvals following community opposition. Concerns over land use incompatibility, limited local employment, and grid and water demands drove the board's decision to pause new data center development.

18057.name: Hamilton County

18097.name: Marion County

19061.description: Dubuque County has discussed data center growth management requirements as data center developers have shown interest in the area. 19113.name: Linn County
19113.title: Linn County 18-Month Data Center Moratorium (July 2026)
19113.description: Linn County Board of Supervisors voted 2-1 on July 1, 2026 to impose an 18-month moratorium on new applications to rezone unincorporated county land to the EU-3 Large-Scale Data Center Zoning District, effective immediately through January 2028. The moratorium follows a February 2026 data center ordinance that required water studies and community benefit agreements, but additional analysis on cumulative impacts to water resources, electrical infrastructure, emergency response, and long-range land use planning prompted the Board to pause new applications while updating regulations. Linn County (Cedar Rapids) had been a premier Iowa data center market hosting Microsoft and Google facilities. Iowa's §423.3 sales-tax exemption on data center equipment remains in effect statewide.
19113.notes: Moratorium runs through January 2028. Iowa's statewide data center tax exemption (§423.3) remains; this moratorium applies only to new rezoning applications in unincorporated Linn County.

sources.label: Linn County, IA — Official moratorium newsflash
Linn County, IA — Data Centers in Unincorporated Linn County (official page)

19153.name: Polk County

19153.description: Polk County (Des Moines / West Des Moines) is Iowa's premier data center market. Microsoft operates a large campus in West Des Moines; Meta has a major facility in Altoona; Google, Aligned, and others have substantial presences. Iowa Code §423.3 eliminates sales and use tax on data center equipment and electricity for qualifying facilities, making Iowa one of the most data center-friendly states in the nation.

32003.name: Clark County
32003.title: Nevada Tax Abatements — Las Vegas Data Center Market
32003.description: Clark County (Las Vegas) is a major data center market anchored by Switch's PYRAMID campus and facilities from DataBank, Evoque, and other operators. Nevada's personal property tax abatements and sales tax exemptions for qualifying data centers (NRS §374.357) combined with no corporate income tax make Clark County a top Sunbelt data center destination.
32003.notes: City of Henderson (within Clark County) proposed a 180-day moratorium on new data center conditional use permits; city council scheduled to vote July 21, 2026. Clark County commissioners signaled intention to develop regulations and consider a freeze on new applications as of July 8, 2026. Neither action is yet enacted; Nevada's tax abatement program remains in effect.

44007.name: Providence County
44007.title: Smithfield Permanent Data Center Ban
44007.description: The Town of Smithfield in Providence County enacted a permanent ban on large-scale data center development in 2023, making it one of the first municipalities in the US to outright prohibit data centers via zoning ordinance. sources: Meta Aiken County, SC data center campus announcement

45015.name: Berkeley County
45015.title: South Carolina Data Center Incentive Zone — Amazon/AWS Hub
45015.description: South Carolina offers some of the strongest data center incentives in the nation: 20-year sales tax exemptions on equipment and 20-year property tax abatements. Berkeley County hosts major Amazon/AWS facilities and has become one of the East Coast's fastest-growing data center markets.
sources: SC Code §12-6-3360 data center incentives; Amazon/AWS data centers, Berkeley County

47001.name: Anderson County
47001.title: Active Moratorium — High-Intensity Computing Restrictions
47001.description: Anderson County (near Oak Ridge / Knoxville) enacted restrictions on new large-scale data centers and high-intensity computing following the xAI Colossus controversy and statewide concerns about TVA grid capacity. The county moratorium reflects concerns about power demand on the TVA transmission system serving eastern Tennessee.

sources.label: Anderson County TN Commission — Data Center Moratorium Resolution (2024)
Knox News: Tennessee counties impose data center moratoriums

sources: Anderson County TN data center moratorium (2024)

47037.name: Davidson County
47037.title: Nashville Data Center Growth Controls
47037.description: Davidson County (Nashville) has proposed regulations on large-scale data centers following rapid growth in applications, strained TVA grid capacity, and community concerns. Nashville's rapid tech sector growth has accelerated data center demand while also raising opposition from community groups focused on land use.

sources.label: Davidson County (Nashville) Metro Council — AI Data Center Policy Discussion
Nashville Business Journal: Data center regulations debate

sources: Davidson County TN Metro Nashville proposed data center regulations (2024)

47065.name: Hamilton County
47065.title: Active Moratorium — Data Center and Crypto Mining Restrictions
47065.description: Hamilton County (Chattanooga) has enacted a moratorium on new large-scale data centers and cryptocurrency mining operations citing TVA grid capacity concerns and community opposition. Chattanooga's EPB fiber network had previously attracted significant data center interest.

sources.label: Hamilton County TN Commission — Data Center Moratorium (2024)
Chattanooga Times Free Press: Hamilton County data center moratorium

47031.name: Coffee County
47031.title: Proposed Moratorium — High-Intensity Computing Restrictions
47031.description: Coffee County (Manchester) is among the Tennessee counties considering moratoriums on large data centers and cryptocurrency mining following the xAI Colossus controversy and statewide TVA capacity concerns in 2024.
sources: Coffee County TN proposed data center restrictions (2024)
Tennessee county data center moratorium wave, 2024

47157.name: Shelby County
47157.title: TVA Power Demand Scrutiny — xAI Colossus Supercomputer Hub
47157.description: Shelby County (Memphis) is home to xAI's Colossus supercomputer facility — the world's largest known GPU cluster as of 2024, drawing approximately 150 MW. The Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) has implemented stricter scrutiny of large power loads in the Memphis area amid rapid demand growth, requiring enhanced interconnection review for new high-density computing facilities.
47157.notes: xAI Colossus Phase 1 launched Sep 2024 with 100,000 H100 GPUs; Phase 2 expansion ongoing.

sources.label: xAI Colossus announcement — Elon Musk / xAI, Sep 2024

sources: TVA Large Power Customer interconnection requirements
Tennessee Code Ann. § 67-6-206 data center sales tax exemption

47179.name: Washington County
47179.title: Active Moratorium — Data Center Development Restrictions
47179.description: Washington County (Johnson City) enacted restrictions on new data center developments in 2024 amid the statewide wave of county-level data center moratoriums. Concerns center on TVA grid capacity, water use, and land use incompatibility with agricultural heritage.

sources.label: Washington County TN Commission — Data Center Moratorium (2024)
Johnson City Press: Washington County data center restrictions

48085.name: Collin County
48085.title: ERCOT Grid Curtailment Requirements
48085.description: Collin County (Allen/Plano/McKinney area) data centers are subject to ERCOT demand response and curtailment requirements.
sources: ERCOT demand response programs; Texas SB 3 (2021)

48113.name: Dallas County
48113.title: ERCOT Grid Curtailment Requirements
48113.description: Dallas-area data centers are subject to ERCOT demand response requirements and potential curtailment during grid emergencies.
sources: ERCOT demand response programs; Texas SB 3 (2021)

48139.name: Ellis County
48139.title: Texas Chapter 312/313 Abatements — Google Midlothian Campus
48139.description: Ellis County (Midlothian) hosts a major Google data center campus serving the Dallas-Fort Worth metro area. Texas's historically generous Chapter 312/313 property tax abatements for large capital investments, combined with no state income tax and ERCOT grid access, have made Ellis County a significant data center hub.
48139.notes: Texas Chapter 313 (the school property tax abatement program) expired Dec 2022 but Chapter 312 abatements remain available through counties.

sources.label: Texas Economic Development — Data Center Incentives

sources: Google Midlothian, TX data center campus; Ellis County Chapter 312 abatements

48201.name: Harris County
48201.title: ERCOT Grid Curtailment and Demand Response Requirements
48201.description: Following Winter Storm Uri (Feb 2021), large power consumers including data centers in ERCOT territory must register for and participate in demand response programs. Data centers face potential curtailment during grid emergencies.
sources: ERCOT demand response programs; Texas SB 3 (2021)

48453.name: Travis County
48453.title: ERCOT Requirements and City of Austin AI Procurement Policy
48453.description: Travis County (Austin) data centers face ERCOT demand response requirements. The City of Austin adopted AI procurement guidelines requiring bias audits and impact assessments for government AI systems.
sources: ERCOT demand response; Austin AI Procurement Policy (2021)

48491.name: Williamson County
48491.title: Texas Incentives — Samsung Taylor Fab + Tech Investment Hub
48491.description: Williamson County (Taylor, Round Rock, Georgetown) is a fast-growing tech hub anchored by Samsung's $17B+ advanced semiconductor fab in Taylor (announced 2021) and major data center investment. Dell Technologies is headquartered in Round Rock. Texas's no-income-tax environment and historically generous Chapter 312 abatements make Williamson County a significant data infrastructure destination.

sources.label: Samsung Taylor, TX chip fab announcement (2021)

49021.name: Iron County
49021.title: Data Center Moratorium — Water and Energy Constraints
49021.description: Iron County (Cedar City) has enacted restrictions and moratoriums on new large-scale data centers and cryptocurrency mining operations, citing acute water scarcity in Utah's arid southwest and grid capacity limitations on Rocky Mountain Power's network.
sources: Iron County UT data center restrictions (2023)

51059.name: Fairfax County
51059.title: Comprehensive Plan Restrictions — Data Center Growth Controls
51059.description: Fairfax County's 2022 Comprehensive Plan amendments impose significant data center restrictions: mandatory utility infrastructure capacity demonstrations, community impact mitigation plans, and firm limits on data center development in residential transition areas. A 2023 county board vote imposed further density caps amid community pressure over visual blight, noise, and grid strain.
51059.notes: County supervisors voted in 2023 to restrict new data center approvals in certain western Fairfax districts.

sources.label: Fairfax County Comprehensive Plan — Data Center Density Policies
Fairfax County Board of Supervisors — Zoning Amendments 2023
Fairfax County Comprehensive Plan Amendment ZOAM 2022

51107.name: Loudoun County
51107.title: Data Center Overlay Zone Restrictions
51107.description: Loudoun County ('Data Center Alley') adopted new zoning rules restricting data center development in rural/western areas and imposing strict density and setback requirements in all zones.

sources.label: Loudoun County Zoning Ordinance Amendment ZOAM-2019-0004
Loudoun County Data Center Policy — Official Page

51153.name: Prince William County
51153.title: Digital Gateway Area Restrictions
51153.description: Prince William County passed restrictions limiting data center density in the Digital Gateway area following sustained community opposition over environmental and land-use impacts.

sources.label: Prince William County — Digital Gateway & Data Center Zoning Policy
Prince William County Board of Supervisors Resolution (2021)

53007.name: Chelan County
53007.title: PUD Moratorium on High-Intensity Computing
53007.description: Chelan County PUD (public utility district) imposed a moratorium on new cryptocurrency mining and high-density computing operations due to electric grid capacity constraints.

sources.label: Chelan County PUD — Rate Structure for Large Electric Loads
WA UTC Order 01 ELEC 3274-T — PUD Cryptocurrency Load Management

sources: Chelan County PUD Board resolution, 2019

53017.name: Douglas County
53017.title: PUD Moratorium on High-Intensity Computing
53017.description: Douglas County PUD imposed capacity restrictions and moratoriums on cryptocurrency mining and high-intensity computing operations due to grid constraints.

sources.label: Douglas County PUD — Large Load Service Policy
WA Utilities and Transportation Commission — Large Load Policies

sources: Douglas County PUD Board resolution, 2019

53025.name: Grant County
53025.title: PUD Capacity Restrictions on High-Power Computing
53025.description: Grant County PUD placed restrictions on high-power computing operations including cryptocurrency mining due to transmission capacity constraints and cost-of-service concerns.

sources.label: Grant County PUD — Electric Service Policy for Cryptocurrency Mining
WA UTC — Utility Rate Cases for Digital Currency Mining

sources: Grant County PUD policy, 2018

53033.name: King County
53033.title: Environmental Review and AI Surveillance Restrictions
53033.description: King County banned government use of facial recognition technology (2021). New data center development requires SEPA (State Environmental Policy Act) review including energy and water impact assessment.
sources: King County Ordinance 19022 (2021); WA SEPA

53047.name: Okanogan County
53047.title: PUD Capacity Restrictions on High-Power Computing
53047.description: Okanogan County PUD imposed capacity-based restrictions on new high-power computing loads including cryptocurrency mining.

sources.label: Okanogan County — PUD Rate Study for High-Load Customers
Okanogan County Commissioner Meetings — Data Center Ordinance

sources: Okanogan County PUD policy

55025.name: Dane County
55025.title: City of Madison Temporary Data Center Moratorium (Jan 2026)
55025.description: Madison's Common Council voted 15-4 on January 13, 2026 to approve a temporary moratorium on issuing zoning approvals for new data centers with a floor area larger than 10,000 sq ft. The moratorium gives city staff time to update zoning codes to address energy demands, water consumption, heat generation, air quality, and land use compatibility with residential neighborhoods. Dane County had already been pursuing environmental review requirements for large data center developments since 2023; the Madison city moratorium extends that protective posture into an active pause on new approvals.
55025.notes: City of Madison moratorium applies to facilities >10,000 sq ft. Dane County environmental review requirements also apply county-wide.

sources.label: City of Madison — Temporary Data Center Moratorium (official page)

55071.name: Manitowoc County
55071.title: Proposed Data Center Restrictions
55071.description: Manitowoc County has considered restrictions on large-scale data center developments following community concerns about land use, limited employment generation, and energy demands on We Energies' grid.

sources.label: Manitowoc County WI Board of Supervisors — Data Center Proposal Review
Herald Times Reporter: Manitowoc County data center discussions

sources: Manitowoc County WI proposed data center zoning restrictions (2024)

55101.name: Racine County
55101.title: Wisconsin Incentives — Microsoft $3.3B AI Campus (Mount Pleasant)
55101.description: Racine County (Mount Pleasant) is the site of Microsoft's announced $3.3 billion AI data center campus, one of the largest tech infrastructure investments in Wisconsin's history. Microsoft announced the project in May 2024. Wisconsin's data center property tax exemption and Racine County economic development incentives apply.
55101.notes: Microsoft announced plans to build the campus in the Foxconn/Wisconsin Innovation Park site, which had been underutilized since Foxconn's scaled-back plans.

sources.label: Microsoft Wisconsin AI Data Center Announcement, May 2024

56021.name: Laramie County
56021.title: Wyoming Digital Asset Regulations
56021.description: While Wyoming has been crypto-friendly at the state level, Laramie County has implemented local land use requirements for large digital asset mining and computing facilities.
sources: Wyoming Digital Asset regulations; Laramie County zoning

18099.name: Marshall County
18099.title: Marshall County Permanent Data Center Ban (April 2026)
18099.description: Marshall County Commissioners enacted a permanent ban on new data centers in April 2026, after a one-year moratorium (February 2025 – February 2026) expired. County leaders reported this may be the first permanent data center ban in Indiana. The ban was accompanied by strict limits on Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), requiring a 1,320-foot setback from adjoining property lines, and first-reading restrictions on large-scale solar farms. Community opposition focused on water availability, noise, limited local employment, and conflicts with the county's rural agricultural character.
18099.notes: Originally a 1-year moratorium (Feb 2025) that was converted to a permanent ban in April 2026. First Indiana county to enact a permanent ban.

sources.label: Marshall County, IN — Official government website
WSBT: Marshall County approves permanent ban on data centers
WNDU: Marshall County commissioners ban data centers

18017.name: Cass County
18017.title: Cass County Permanent Data Center Ban (2026)
18017.description: Cass County Commissioners voted to ban new data centers entirely in 2026, making Cass one of only two Indiana counties (alongside Marshall) to enact a permanent prohibition rather than a temporary moratorium. The vote was reported in April 2026, with commissioners stating clearly there would be no data centers in the county. Community concerns mirrored other Indiana counties: water resource impacts, limited local employment, energy infrastructure strain, and incompatibility with the county's existing industrial and agricultural land uses.
18017.notes: One of two Indiana counties with permanent ban (alongside Marshall County). Exact ordinance number not confirmed from official source; verify at casscounty.in.gov.

sources.label: Cass County, IN — Official government website
Kokomo Tribune: Cass County officials vote against allowing data centers
WFYI: Nearly a third of Indiana counties restrict data centers

18011.name: Boone County
18011.title: Boone County 1-Year Data Center Moratorium (June 2026)
18011.description: Boone County Commissioners voted unanimously on June 15, 2026 to impose a one-year moratorium on new data center development in unincorporated Boone County, effective June 16, 2026. The moratorium pauses filing, processing, and acceptance of applications for new data center facilities while the county updates its Comprehensive Plan and reviews zoning regulations. Boone County's Area Plan Commission unanimously recommended the moratorium following discussions about data center impacts on land use, infrastructure, utilities, agriculture, and the county's rural character. Boone County is part of the Indianapolis metropolitan area and had attracted significant data center interest.
18011.notes: Moratorium runs June 16, 2026 – June 16, 2027. Applies to unincorporated Boone County. 12th Indiana county to implement a moratorium.

sources.label: Boone County, IN — Official moratorium announcement (boonecounty.in.gov)
WTHR: Boone County commissioners approve moratorium on data centers
IBJ: Boone County passes moratorium on data center development

18181.name: White County
18181.title: White County Data Center Moratorium (October 2025)
18181.description: White County Board of Commissioners passed a moratorium on data center development on October 20, 2025, with Commissioner Mike Smolek the lone dissenting vote. The moratorium followed packed public meetings where residents expressed opposition based on concerns about water availability — Monticello-area aquifers were identified as a key constraint — and lack of transparency in the development review process. White County was one of the earliest Indiana counties to act after Marshall County, and the IBJ identified it among the first three to impose a moratorium specifically targeting data centers.
18181.notes: One of first three Indiana counties to impose a data center-specific moratorium (alongside Marshall and Putnam). Water availability in Monticello area was a primary concern.

sources.label: White County, IN — Official government website
IBJ: 3 counties put moratoriums on data center projects
Daily Journal: 3 counties put moratoriums on data center projects

18133.name: Putnam County
18133.title: Putnam County Data Center Moratorium (November 2025)
18133.description: Putnam County enacted a 1-year moratorium on data centers (along with wind energy, solar, and small modular reactors) effective November 1, 2025. Putnam County was one of the three earliest Indiana counties to impose a data-center-specific moratorium, alongside Marshall and White counties. The broad moratorium covering multiple energy infrastructure types reflects community concerns about large-scale industrial development and its effects on Putnam County's rural character, agricultural land, and local infrastructure capacity.
18133.notes: 1-year moratorium covers data centers, wind, solar, and SMRs. Effective November 2025. One of first three Indiana counties to act.

sources.label: Putnam County, IN — Official government website
IBJ: 3 counties put moratoriums on data center projects
WFYI: Nearly a third of Indiana counties restrict data centers

48209.name: Hays County
48209.title: San Marcos Data Center Ban + Hays County Development Pause (2026)
48209.description: San Marcos City Council voted 4-3 on June 16, 2026 to ban data centers from all zoning categories within city limits, becoming the first Texas city to implement such a ban. The city cited water and energy resource conflicts with local community needs. At the county level, the Hays County Commission adopted a non-binding resolution to pause data center development in unincorporated areas due to water shortages — at least two large data center projects (including the 1.2 GW CloudBurst San Marcos GigaCenter spanning Hays and Guadalupe counties) had been proposed in the unincorporated county surrounding San Marcos. The San Marcos ban faces potential legal challenges under Texas' 2023 Death Star preemption law and 2025 HB 2559, which restricts indefinite municipal moratoriums.
48209.notes: City of San Marcos enacted an outright ban (Tier 4 at city level). Hays County Commission resolution is non-binding. Both face potential preemption challenges under Texas law.

sources.label: Texas Tribune: San Marcos bans data centers, testing its local control
The Real Deal Texas: San Marcos data center ban — legal challenges
Yahoo News: Zoning authority — San Marcos first Texas city to ban data centers

51061.name: Fauquier County
51061.title: Temporary Moratorium on Data Center Rezoning Applications
51061.description: In September 2023 the Fauquier County Board of Supervisors enacted a temporary moratorium on accepting data center rezoning applications while the county updated its Comprehensive Plan to address land-use, visual, noise, and grid-impact concerns. The moratorium applied to the agricultural and rural buffer districts that make up most of the county. A revised data-center-specific zoning chapter was required before new applications would be accepted.
51061.notes: Moratorium enacted amid strong community opposition to proposed rural campuses near historic areas.

sources.label: Fauquier County Board of Supervisors — Official Meeting Agendas & Minutes
Fauquier County Planning Department — Comprehensive Plan Updates

51047.name: Culpeper County
51047.title: Data Center Siting Review Requirements
51047.description: Culpeper County has emerged as a secondary data center market in the Virginia corridor as land costs and grid constraints push development west from Loudoun. The county requires environmental review and community input for new data center rezonings, particularly near the Rapidan River watershed and in agricultural areas. Light regulations currently; county is actively evaluating policy updates.
51047.notes: Several hundred acres of data center campus proposals under review as of 2024.

51177.name: Spotsylvania County
51177.title: Data Center Overlay District — Density and Energy Caps
51177.description: Spotsylvania County adopted a Data Center Overlay District following rapid growth along the Interstate 95 corridor. The overlay imposes density caps, mandatory 100% renewable power demonstration, noise ordinances, and setback requirements from residential areas. The Spotsylvania Solar Farm controversy highlighted grid capacity concerns, prompting additional power-use review for large data centers.
51177.notes: Board of Supervisors tightened restrictions in 2023 after residents raised concerns about grid load.

41067.name: Washington County
41067.title: Pro Data Center — Major Semiconductor & Cloud Infrastructure Hub
41067.description: Washington County (Hillsboro) is Oregon's largest data center market and home to Intel's primary US manufacturing campus, Amazon Web Services, Google, Meta, and numerous colocation facilities. The county actively courts technology investment through Oregon's property tax Enterprise Zones, which exempt qualifying data centers from property taxes for up to 15 years. No restrictions on data center development; expedited permitting for qualifying facilities.
41067.notes: Oregon Enterprise Zone and Strategic Investment Program provide strong incentives.

sources.label: Oregon Enterprise Zone Program — Oregon Dept. of Revenue

41051.name: Multnomah County
41051.title: Environmental Review Requirements for Large Data Centers
41051.description: Multnomah County (Portland) applies standard Type III land-use review for large data centers over 100,000 sq ft in mixed/industrial zones, requiring public notice and environmental review. Portland's climate emergency declaration and 2050 carbon-neutral goals add scrutiny to grid-intensive facilities. Several colocation operators maintain facilities here (Internap/Flexential, Zayo) but new large campuses typically locate in Washington County or further east.
41051.notes: Portland Bureau of Development Services applies standard environmental review; no outright ban.

sources.label: Portland Bureau of Development Services — Land Use Review

32019.name: Lyon County
32019.title: Pro Data Center — Tahoe-Reno Industrial Center (TRIC) Hub
32019.description: Lyon County is home to the Tahoe-Reno Industrial Center (TRIC), one of the world's largest industrial parks, hosting hyperscale data centers for Apple, Google, and Switch, as well as the Tesla Gigafactory. The county offers substantial tax incentives including sales and use tax abatements under Nevada Revised Statutes Chapter 360. Low energy costs, a dry climate ideal for air cooling, and minimal regulatory burden make Lyon County a top-tier data center destination.
32019.notes: Nevada's Chapter 360 incentive program offers sales tax abatement for qualifying capital investments.

sources.label: Tahoe Reno Industrial Center — Official Site
Nevada Governor's Office of Economic Development — Data Centers
Nevada Revised Statutes Chapter 360 — Tax Abatement for Data Centers

16001.name: Ada County
16001.title: Pro Data Center — Boise-Area Technology Hub
16001.description: Ada County (Boise) is Idaho's primary technology hub and hosts significant data center infrastructure including Meta's 1.9-million sq ft Eagle data center campus, Micron Technology's HPC/AI computing infrastructure, and numerous regional operators. Idaho Power provides low-cost hydroelectric power, and the state offers sales tax exemptions for data center equipment under Idaho Code §63-3622O. The Treasure Valley region is one of the fastest-growing data center markets in the Mountain West.
16001.notes: Idaho Code §63-3622O exempts data center equipment and power from sales tax.

sources.label: Idaho State Tax Commission — Data Center Exemption (IC §63-3622O)
Meta Eagle Data Center — Meta Infrastructure

21111.name: Jefferson County
21111.title: Pro Data Center — Louisville Technology Hub
21111.description: Jefferson County (Louisville) is Kentucky's primary data center market, home to Amazon Web Services, Expedient, CyrusOne, and numerous enterprise facilities. Louisville Electric (LG&E) provides competitive power rates, and Kentucky offers a data center incentive program under KRS 139.517 exempting qualifying equipment and electricity from sales and use tax. Louisville's central US location, fiber infrastructure, and business-friendly environment continue to attract new investment.
21111.notes: KRS 139.517 provides sales tax exemption for qualifying data center investments over $100M.

sources.label: Kentucky Cabinet for Economic Development — Data Centers
Kentucky Revised Statutes 139.517 — Data Center Tax Exemption

29095.name: Jackson County
29095.title: Pro Data Center — Kansas City Technology Corridor
29095.description: Jackson County (Kansas City) anchors Missouri's largest data center market. QTS Data Centers, Evergy's cloud-anchored facilities, and major enterprise operators maintain significant infrastructure here. Missouri's Chapter 353 and Enhanced Enterprise Zone programs provide property tax abatements. Kansas City's central US geography, diverse fiber routes, and 100Gbps peering at Kansas City Internet Exchange (KCIX) make it a strategic hub.
29095.notes: Missouri Chapters 353 and 100 tax abatement programs available for qualifying data centers.

sources.label: Missouri Department of Economic Development — Incentives

29189.name: St. Louis County
29189.title: Pro Data Center — St. Louis Metro Technology Hub
29189.description: St. Louis County is Missouri's secondary data center market, home to Lumen Technologies (formerly CenturyLink) network operations, Windstream, Cologix, and enterprise facilities serving the regional financial sector. The county benefits from Missouri's Chapter 353 Urban Redevelopment program and Brownfields incentives for qualifying technology developments.

sources.label: Missouri Department of Economic Development — Incentives

40109.name: Oklahoma County
40109.title: Pro Data Center — Oklahoma City Growing Hub
40109.description: Oklahoma County (Oklahoma City) is a rapidly growing data center market driven by low power costs (OG&E and OEC Fiber), land availability, and Oklahoma's data center incentive under 68 O.S. §1359.2, which provides a full sales tax exemption on data center equipment and power for facilities meeting a capital investment threshold. Microsoft has announced multi-billion dollar data center investments in Oklahoma, with facilities in the OKC metro.
40109.notes: 68 O.S. §1359.2 provides full sales and use tax exemption for qualifying data centers.

sources.label: Oklahoma Tax Commission — Data Center Exemption (68 O.S. §1359.2)
Oklahoma Department of Commerce — Data Centers

35001.name: Bernalillo County
35001.title: Pro Data Center — Albuquerque Technology Hub
35001.description: Bernalillo County (Albuquerque) is New Mexico's largest data center market. Meta operates a large data center campus in the county, and Microsoft has announced data center investments in the Albuquerque metro. New Mexico offers gross receipts tax deductions for data center equipment under NMSA §7-9-57, plus property tax abatements through the county. The state's renewable energy resources (wind and solar) and moderate climate attract sustainability-focused operators.
35001.notes: NMSA §7-9-57 provides gross receipts tax deduction for qualified data center equipment.

sources.label: New Mexico Taxation and Revenue Department — Data Center GRT Deduction
New Mexico Economic Development — Tech Industries

12057.name: Hillsborough County
12057.title: Pro Data Center — Tampa Bay Technology Hub
12057.description: Hillsborough County (Tampa) hosts a growing data center cluster including DataSite (QTS), Evoque, Cologix, and enterprise colocation operators. Florida's 2023 HB 7063 expanded sales tax exemptions for data centers meeting a $150M capital investment threshold, providing 20-year exemptions on equipment and electricity. The Tampa metro's hurricane-resilient construction standards, diverse fiber routes, and proximity to LatAm traffic make it a strategic East Coast / Caribbean hub.
12057.notes: Florida HB 7063 (2023) provides significant sales tax exemptions for qualifying data centers.

sources.label: Florida Department of Revenue — Data Center Exemptions (HB 7063)

12086.name: Miami-Dade County
12086.title: Environmental Resilience Review — Coastal Climate Regulations
12086.description: Miami-Dade County applies coastal resilience and sea-level rise review to large developments including data centers in flood-prone zones. The county's 2021 Sea Level Rise Strategy requires climate vulnerability assessments for major infrastructure. Several colocation operators (Equinix, Verizon NAP of the Americas, DataSite) maintain facilities here. The county is pro-business but the added environmental review layer classifies it as Level 1.
12086.notes: NAP of the Americas (Verizon/Terremark) is a major LatAm internet hub in downtown Miami.

sources.label: Miami-Dade County Sea Level Rise Strategy (2021)
Miami-Dade Department of Regulatory and Economic Resources

48121.name: Denton County
48121.title: Pro Data Center — DFW North Corridor Hub
48121.description: Denton County is part of the rapidly expanding Dallas-Fort Worth data center corridor, particularly along Interstate 35 and the US-380 tech corridor. Flexential, Stream Data Centers, and various enterprise operators have facilities here. Texas provides data centers with a sales and use tax exemption under Texas Tax Code §151.359 for qualifying facilities. Abundant land, low commercial power rates (ERCOT), and proximity to DFW airport make this a high-growth submarket.
48121.notes: Texas Tax Code §151.359 exempts data center equipment from sales tax for qualifying operators.

48441.name: Taylor County
48441.title: Pro Data Center — Stargate / OpenAI AI Campus Hub (Abilene)
48441.description: Taylor County (Abilene) was selected in 2025 as the initial site for the Stargate AI infrastructure project, a joint venture of OpenAI, SoftBank, Oracle, and MGX targeting $500 billion in US AI infrastructure investment. The Abilene campus is the first ground-up Stargate data center, occupying hundreds of acres adjacent to ERCOT transmission infrastructure. AEP Texas provides power. The county and City of Abilene offered significant tax and land-use incentives to secure the project.
48441.notes: Stargate joint venture announced January 2025; Abilene selected as first campus site.

sources.label: City of Abilene Economic Development Corporation

31109.name: Lancaster County
31109.title: Pro Data Center — Lincoln Technology Campus Hub
31109.description: Lancaster County (Lincoln) is Nebraska's secondary data center market after Omaha (Douglas County). Lincoln Electric System (LES) provides some of the lowest commercial power rates in the Midwest. Nebraska offers a personal property tax exemption for data center equipment under LB 1031. Ameritas Life Partners and various regional operators maintain data centers in the Lincoln area.
31109.notes: Nebraska LB 1031 exempts qualifying data center equipment from personal property tax.

sources.label: Nebraska Department of Revenue — Data Center Exemptions

53005.name: Benton County
53005.title: Pro Data Center — Columbia Basin Cheap Power Hub (Kennewick)
53005.description: Benton County (Kennewick) is part of Washington's Columbia Basin data center cluster, benefiting from hydroelectric power from Bonneville Power Administration at among the lowest industrial rates in North America (~2-3 cents/kWh). The Tri-Cities area (Kennewick, Pasco, Richland) hosts several hyperscale facilities and the Pacific Northwest National Laboratory (PNNL) computing infrastructure. The county has no restrictions on data center development.
53005.notes: BPA hydroelectric power provides some of the lowest industrial electricity rates in the US.

53021.name: Franklin County
53021.title: Pro Data Center — Pasco / Tri-Cities Hydropower Hub
53021.description: Franklin County (Pasco) shares the Columbia Basin hydroelectric power advantage with adjacent Benton and Grant counties. The Port of Pasco's industrial land and BPA power access have attracted data center operators seeking renewable, low-cost electricity. Apple operates a significant data center campus in the Tri-Cities area. The county offers no restrictions on data center development.
53021.notes: Apple has operated a large data center in the Tri-Cities area since approximately 2012.

13151.name: Henry County
13151.title: Rapid Data Center Growth — Infrastructure Strain Review
13151.description: Henry County, south of Atlanta, has experienced rapid data center expansion along the I-75 corridor. The county has imposed mandatory grid-impact assessments and stormwater management requirements following complaints about infrastructure stress from large-scale development. Georgia Power's load growth attributed to data centers in Henry County has prompted review of new substation interconnection timelines. Several rezoning applications face extended review periods as a result.
13151.notes: Georgia Power load studies required for data centers >10MW load in Henry County.

37109.name: Lincoln County
37109.title: Pro Data Center — Duke Energy Catawba River Corridor
37109.description: Lincoln County (Lincolnton) is part of North Carolina's emerging western Piedmont data center corridor, benefiting from Duke Energy's robust transmission infrastructure along the Catawba River. The county's industrial land availability, NC's Article 3F investment tax credit, and competitive power rates have attracted several data center proposals. The county is actively promoting the area through economic development incentives.
37109.notes: NC Article 3F provides income tax credit for qualifying data center investments over $75M.

sources.label: NC Department of Commerce — Article 3F Data Center Incentive

42095.name: Northampton County
42095.title: Lehigh Valley Data Center Growth — Standard Environmental Review
42095.description: Northampton County (Bethlehem) is part of Pennsylvania's Lehigh Valley data center corridor alongside Lehigh County. The region has attracted QTS, Iron Mountain, and regional colocation operators. Pennsylvania applies standard Act 537 sewage facility planning and stormwater management review for large developments. No specific data center restrictions; the review requirements are the same as for other large industrial developments.
42095.notes: Lehigh Valley Economic Development Corporation actively markets the corridor to data center operators.

sources.label: PA Dept. of Environmental Protection — Act 537 Planning

42011.name: Berks County
42011.title: Pro Data Center — Reading / Berks County Digital Corridor
42011.description: Berks County (Reading) has emerged as a cost-competitive alternative to the Northern Virginia and NJ/NY data center markets. Iron Mountain, NorthStar, and regional operators have facilities here. PPL Electric's transmission infrastructure, available land, and Pennsylvania's Keystone Opportunity Zones provide competitive economics. The county actively recruits data center investment.
42011.notes: Pennsylvania Keystone Opportunity Zones can provide significant tax relief for qualifying facilities.

sources.label: Pennsylvania Department of Community & Economic Development — KOZ Program

45063.name: Lexington County
45063.title: Pro Data Center — Columbia Metro Hub
45063.description: Lexington County (Cayce/West Columbia) is part of South Carolina's Columbia metro data center market. SC's Chapter 4A (§12-6-3375 through §12-6-3380) provides income tax credits and special source revenue credits for qualifying data centers investing over $50M. SCE&G (Dominion Energy SC) power, available industrial land, and the state's competitive incentive environment have attracted several operators to the region.
45063.notes: SC §12-6-3375 provides income tax credits for qualifying data centers.

sources.label: SC Department of Commerce — Data Center Incentives

26125.name: Oakland County
26125.title: Pro Data Center — Detroit Metro Hub
26125.description: Oakland County (Pontiac, Troy) is Michigan's primary data center market, hosting Switch's SUPERNAP Michigan facility and numerous enterprise operators serving the Detroit metro's automotive and financial sector. Michigan's PA 328 (2014) provides sales and use tax exemptions for data center equipment investments over $25M. The county actively recruits technology investment through Oakland County's One Stop Shop permitting program.
26125.notes: Michigan PA 328 (2014) provides sales and use tax exemptions for qualifying data center investments.

sources.label: Michigan Economic Development Corporation — Data Center Incentives
Michigan PA 328 (2014) — Data Center Tax Exemption

01089.name: Madison County
01089.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Meta Huntsville Campus
01089.description: Madison County is home to Meta's Huntsville data center campus, one of the largest in the southeastern US. Alabama awarded a multi-hundred-million-dollar incentive package under the Alabama Jobs Act. The county and city of Huntsville maintain a strongly favorable posture toward large hyperscale investment.

sources.label: Alabama Department of Commerce — Meta Huntsville Announcement

17037.name: DeKalb County
17037.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Meta DeKalb Campus
17037.description: Meta Platforms operates a large data center campus in DeKalb, Illinois, representing an investment of approximately $800 million. The county actively recruited the facility with enterprise zone incentives. Illinois law provides a 5-year tax credit of up to $250M for qualifying data center investments.

sources.label: Illinois Data Center Tax Credit — IDOR

19049.name: Dallas County
19049.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Apple Waukee Campus
19049.description: Apple operates a $1.3 billion data center campus in Waukee, Dallas County, Iowa. The facility is Apple's second Iowa data center and among its largest in North America. Iowa's data center sales tax exemption applies to qualifying equipment. Waukee city and Dallas County granted significant local incentives.

sources.label: Apple — Waukee, Iowa Data Center Expansion
Iowa Economic Development Authority — Apple Waukee Project

31153.name: Sarpy County
31153.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Google Papillion Campus
31153.description: Google operates a major data center campus in Papillion, Sarpy County, Nebraska. The multi-building campus represents a multi-billion-dollar investment and is one of Google's largest facilities in the central US. Nebraska's ImagiNE Act provides sales tax exemptions on data center equipment. Sarpy County actively supports data center development.

sources.label: Nebraska ImagiNE Act — Data Center Incentives

35061.name: Valencia County
35061.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Meta Los Lunas Campus
35061.description: Meta Platforms operates a major data center campus in Los Lunas, Valencia County, New Mexico. The campus represents a $1 billion+ investment and is one of Meta's largest western US facilities. New Mexico's data center gross receipts tax deduction (NMSA §7-9-57) applies. The county and village of Los Lunas granted significant local incentives.

sources.label: Meta — Los Lunas, New Mexico Data Center
New Mexico NMSA §7-9-57 — Gross Receipts Tax Deduction

40097.name: Mayes County
40097.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Google Pryor Creek Campus
40097.description: Google's Pryor Creek data center campus in Mayes County is among the largest data center campuses in the United States, representing a multi-billion-dollar investment. The campus is located in the MidAmerica Industrial Park in Pryor, OK. Oklahoma's data center sales and use tax exemption (68 O.S. §1359.2) applies. Mayes County and the state of Oklahoma have been consistently supportive of data center investment.

sources.label: Google — Pryor Creek, Oklahoma Data Center
Oklahoma Tax Commission — Data Center Exemption §1359.2

41013.name: Crook County
41013.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Apple Prineville Campus
41013.description: Apple operates a major data center campus in Prineville, Crook County, Oregon, representing approximately $1 billion in capital investment. The campus uses renewable energy and is Apple's only company-owned data center in the western US. Oregon's data center enterprise zone incentives apply. Crook County and the City of Prineville granted significant local property tax exemptions.

sources.label: Oregon Enterprise Zone Program — Crook County

47125.name: Montgomery County
47125.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Amazon AWS Clarksville
47125.description: Amazon Web Services operates a large data center campus in Clarksville, Montgomery County, Tennessee. The facility benefits from Tennessee's sales tax exemption on data center equipment and the state's competitive power rates via TVA. Montgomery County and the City of Clarksville have actively recruited data center investment with local property tax incentives.

sources.label: Tennessee Department of Revenue — Data Center Exemption

47165.name: Sumner County
47165.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Meta Gallatin Campus
47165.description: Meta Platforms operates a large data center campus in Gallatin, Sumner County, Tennessee, representing approximately $800 million in capital investment. The campus benefits from Tennessee's data center sales tax exemption. Sumner County and the City of Gallatin provided local property tax incentives.

sources.label: Tennessee Department of Revenue — Data Center Exemptions

48029.name: Bexar County
48029.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Microsoft Lone Star Campus
48029.description: Microsoft operates a major data center campus in San Antonio (Bexar County), Texas, known as the Lone Star Campus. It is one of Microsoft's largest data center campuses globally with multiple buildings and ongoing expansion. Texas Chapter 313 tax abatement agreements (since replaced by Chapter 403 TEXAS Jobs and Security Act) supported the investment. CPS Energy supplies power.

sources.label: Microsoft — San Antonio Data Center Campus
City of San Antonio / CPS Energy — Data Center Partnership

51139.name: Page County
51139.title: Data Center Moratorium Enacted — Luray Caverns Proximity Concerns
51139.description: Page County, Virginia enacted a moratorium on new data center development in 2023 following a proposed Google data center near the nationally significant Luray Caverns. Residents and preservationists raised concerns about groundwater impacts on the caves, noise, power infrastructure, and visual character of the historic Shenandoah Valley landscape. The moratorium halted all new data center permit applications pending a comprehensive use-policy study.
51139.notes: Moratorium enacted pending comprehensive plan amendment; Google withdrew its application.

sources.label: Page County Board of Supervisors — Data Center Moratorium
Shenandoah Valley News — Page County Data Center Moratorium

51157.name: Rappahannock County
51157.title: Data Centers Effectively Prohibited — Comprehensive Plan Amendment
51157.description: Rappahannock County, Virginia amended its Comprehensive Plan and zoning ordinances to effectively prohibit large-scale data centers from locating in the county. The county, which is rural and borders Shenandoah National Park, cited concerns about noise, light pollution, groundwater use, power transmission lines, and incompatibility with the agricultural and scenic character of the county. No data center permit applications are accepted.
51157.notes: Comprehensive Plan explicitly designates data centers as incompatible with county land-use goals.

51171.name: Shenandoah County
51171.title: Data Center Restrictions Under Study — Shenandoah Valley Growth Pressure
51171.description: Shenandoah County has experienced growing pressure from data center developers seeking to locate near the data center corridor. The county board has directed staff to study zoning amendments to limit or control data center siting following significant public opposition. Residents have raised concerns about power transmission infrastructure, water use from the aquifer, and impacts on the valley's scenic and agricultural character.
51171.notes: Restrictions under study; no formal moratorium enacted as of 2024.

sources.label: Shenandoah County Board of Supervisors — Land Use Study
Northern Virginia Daily — Shenandoah Data Center Concerns

08001.name: Adams County
08001.title: Pro Data Center / AI Campus — Adams County Metro Corridor
08001.description: Adams County, north of Denver, hosts Crusoe Energy's AI cloud campus and is part of the I-76 technology corridor. Colorado's OEDIT Advanced Industry Grant program supports qualifying capital investments. Adams County has actively pursued data center and clean-energy computing investment and maintains a favorable land-use posture for hyperscale facilities.

sources.label: Crusoe Energy — Denver Metro AI Campus

10003.name: New Castle County
10003.title: Major Colocation Hub — Wilmington / New Castle Data Center Corridor
10003.description: New Castle County hosts a significant colocation data center cluster in the greater Wilmington area, including facilities operated by Iron Mountain, NTT, and Digital Realty. Delaware's favorable corporate tax environment and proximity to the Philadelphia-Baltimore-DC corridor make it an attractive co-location destination. No state-level data center restrictions exist.

sources.label: Iron Mountain — Wilmington, Delaware Data Center
Delaware Division of Corporations — Business-Friendly Environment

17043.name: DuPage County
17043.title: Major AI / Data Center Hub — Argonne + CoreWeave DuPage Corridor
17043.description: DuPage County hosts Argonne National Laboratory — home to the Aurora exascale supercomputer, one of the world's most powerful AI/HPC systems — as well as a CoreWeave AI computing campus. The county benefits from Illinois's data center investment tax credit (up to $250M over 5 years for qualifying investments). DuPage is a key node in the Chicago data center corridor, with fiber density and redundant power from ComEd.
17043.notes: Home to DOE's Argonne National Laboratory (Aurora supercomputer, 2 exaflops).

sources.label: Illinois DCEO — Data Center Tax Credit

13157.name: Jackson County
13157.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Stanton Springs Technology Park
13157.description: Jackson County is home to the Stanton Springs Technology Park, one of Georgia's premier hyperscale data center destinations. Meta Platforms operates a major data center campus there. The park was developed with state and local incentives including the Georgia Data Center Tax Exemption and significant utility infrastructure investments by Georgia Power.
13157.notes: Stanton Springs is a joint economic development initiative of Jackson, Morgan, Newton, and Walton counties.

sources.label: Meta — Stanton Springs, Georgia Data Center
Georgia Department of Economic Development — Stanton Springs

18063.name: Hendricks County
18063.title: Major AI Campus — Microsoft Hendricks County Hyperscale Cluster
18063.description: Hendricks County, west of Indianapolis, hosts a Microsoft hyperscale AI data center campus. Indiana's EDGE Tax Credit and IT equipment sales tax exemption apply to qualifying capital investments. The county benefits from competitive power rates from Duke Energy Indiana and access to fiber infrastructure along the I-70 corridor. Indiana has actively recruited data center and AI investment.

sources.label: IEDC — Indiana EDGE Tax Credit Program

26081.name: Kent County
26081.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Switch SUPERNAP Michigan
26081.description: Kent County (Grand Rapids area) hosts the Switch SUPERNAP Michigan campus, one of the largest data center campuses in the Midwest. Michigan's PA 328 sales and use tax exemption for data center equipment applies. Kent County actively supports data center investment and Switch has announced continued expansion of the Grand Rapids campus.

sources.label: Switch — Grand Rapids, Michigan SUPERNAP Campus
Michigan PA 328 — Data Center Sales Tax Exemption

27037.name: Dakota County
27037.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Twin Cities South Metro Corridor
27037.description: Dakota County (Eagan / Burnsville area) is the core of the Twin Cities data center market, hosting major facilities from CyrusOne, Compass Datacenters, and regional operators. Minnesota's data center sales tax exemption (Minn. Stat. §297A.68, Subd. 42) applies to qualifying equipment. Eagan's fiber infrastructure and Xcel Energy power access make it the dominant data center submarket in the upper Midwest.

sources.label: Minnesota Dept. of Revenue — Data Center Exemption §297A.68

38015.name: Burleigh County
38015.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Microsoft North Dakota Campus
38015.description: Burleigh County (Bismarck area) hosts a Microsoft hyperscale data center campus, one of the largest data center investments in North Dakota history. North Dakota's NDCC §57-02-08.31 property tax exemption and cold climate (reducing cooling costs) make the state an attractive hyperscale destination. Basin Electric and MDU Resources provide reliable power supply.

sources.label: Microsoft — North Dakota Data Center Investment
North Dakota NDCC §57-02-08.31 — Data Center Exemption

34027.name: Morris County
34027.title: Major AI Campus — CoreWeave Parsippany AI Computing Cluster
34027.description: Morris County hosts the CoreWeave AI computing campus in Parsippany, one of the largest GPU cloud computing clusters on the East Coast. CoreWeave operates thousands of NVIDIA H100/H200 GPUs serving AI model training workloads. The facility benefits from PSE&G power infrastructure and dense fiber connectivity in the New York metro data center corridor. New Jersey's UEZ tax reductions apply to qualifying purchases.

sources.label: CoreWeave — Parsippany, New Jersey AI Campus
New Jersey UEZ Program — Morris County

37067.name: Forsyth County
37067.title: Pro Data Center — Winston-Salem Data Center Corridor
37067.description: Forsyth County (Winston-Salem) hosts a growing data center cluster, including facilities from Windstream, Lumos Networks, and regional colocation operators. The county benefits from North Carolina's data center tax incentive (N.C.G.S. §105-164.13(55)) and Duke Energy Carolinas power rates. Winston-Salem has actively recruited data center investment as part of its technology sector growth strategy.

sources.label: NC Department of Revenue — Data Center Tax Incentive §105-164.13

47093.name: Knox County
47093.title: Pro AI / HPC Hub — ORNL Ecosystem and Knoxville Tech Corridor
47093.description: Knox County anchors the Oak Ridge / Knoxville technology corridor, adjacent to Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) — home to Frontier, the world's first exascale supercomputer, and Inca, its successor. The corridor benefits from TVA's low power rates, the University of Tennessee at Knoxville's research ecosystem, and Tennessee's data center sales tax exemption. The region is actively pursuing follow-on commercial AI computing investment near the DOE complex.
47093.notes: Frontier (ORNL) became the world's first exascale computer in 2022.

sources.label: Oak Ridge National Laboratory — Frontier Supercomputer
Tennessee Valley Authority — Power for Computing

47187.name: Williamson County
47187.title: Growing Data Center Concerns — Franklin / Brentwood Area Opposition
47187.description: Williamson County (Franklin / Brentwood) has seen organized resident opposition to data center proposals in or adjacent to its upscale suburban communities. Planning commission meetings in 2023–2024 included public comment opposing data center rezoning requests near residential neighborhoods, citing traffic, noise, and visual impact. No formal moratorium has been enacted, but developer proposals have faced significant community pushback.
47187.notes: No formal moratorium; community opposition at planning commission level as of 2024.

sources.label: Williamson County Planning Commission — Meeting Records
Williamson Home Page / Tennessee Lookout — Franklin Data Center Opposition

48439.name: Tarrant County
48439.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Meta Fort Worth Campus
48439.description: Tarrant County (Fort Worth / Arlington area) hosts Meta's Fort Worth AI data center campus, one of Meta's largest North American facilities. Texas Chapter 313 tax abatement agreements and the successor Chapter 403 TEXAS Jobs and Security Act have supported the investment. Oncor Electric provides power; Tarrant County's access to renewable wind energy from West Texas makes it attractive for large-scale compute workloads.

sources.label: Meta — Fort Worth, Texas Data Center

49035.name: Salt Lake County
49035.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — Salt Lake City Metro Corridor
49035.description: Salt Lake County hosts a growing hyperscale data center corridor including facilities from Google, C7 Data Centers, Novva Data Centers, and others. Google announced a $1 billion+ data center investment in the Salt Lake metro area in 2024. Utah's sales tax exemption for data center equipment (Utah Code Ann. §59-12-104(77)) and competitive Rocky Mountain Power rates make the state one of the fastest-growing data center markets in the West.
49035.notes: Google announced $1B+ Utah data center investment in 2024.

sources.label: Google — Utah Data Center Investment Announcement 2024
Utah Code Ann. §59-12-104(77) — Data Center Sales Tax Exemption

51087.name: Henrico County
51087.title: Major Data Center Investment Hub — QTS Richmond Campus
51087.description: Henrico County hosts the QTS (now Iron Mountain Data Centers) Richmond hyperscale campus, one of the largest data center campuses in the mid-Atlantic. The facility spans multiple buildings and is one of QTS's flagship campuses. Virginia's Data Center Investment Grant and sales tax exemption for qualifying equipment apply. Henrico County has consistently supported data center investment with local economic development incentives.
51087.notes: QTS Richmond was acquired by Blackstone in 2021; rebranded under Iron Mountain Data Centers.

sources.label: Iron Mountain / QTS — Richmond, Virginia Data Center
Virginia VEDP — Data Center Investment Grant Program

46099.name: Minnehaha County
46099.title: Pro Data Center — Sioux Falls Data Center Hub
46099.description: Minnehaha County (Sioux Falls) has attracted data center investment driven by South Dakota's zero corporate income tax, low property taxes, and competitive electrical rates from Xcel Energy and other utilities. Major data center operators with Sioux Falls facilities include T-Mobile, Midcontinent Communications, and others. The city has actively marketed its data center-friendly environment.
46099.notes: South Dakota has no corporate income tax, a key data center attraction.

sources.label: South Dakota GOED — No Corporate Income Tax
Sioux Falls Development Foundation — Technology Sector

06065.name: Riverside County
06065.title: Major Data Center Corridor — Riverside / Inland Empire
06065.description: Riverside County anchors the Inland Empire data center corridor along I-15 and I-10. QTS Data Centers (now Digital Realty), Aligned Energy, and several wholesale colocation operators run hyperscale facilities in Norco and Mira Loma. California's favorable climate for air-side economization and lower land costs than the Bay Area have attracted sustained capital investment. The county's Riverside County Economic Development Agency actively recruits data center operators with streamlined permitting through the Select Riverside program.
06065.notes: Southern California Edison provides power; CalISO grid region.

sources.label: Digital Realty — Riverside County Data Center
Riverside County EDA — Select Riverside Program

06071.name: San Bernardino County
06071.title: Data Center Water-Use Review — Inland Empire Groundwater Basin
06071.description: San Bernardino County hosts hyperscale data centers in Rialto, Fontana, and Victorville but faces increasing scrutiny over groundwater withdrawals from the Inland Empire Groundwater Basin. The Inland Empire Utilities Agency and local water districts imposed enhanced review requirements for large-scale evaporative cooling systems in 2022 after projections showed data center expansion could strain adjudicated basin allocations. Apple operates a large renewable-powered facility in the county. New facilities must submit a Water Supply Assessment for cooling-water use exceeding 25 acre-feet per year.
06071.notes: WSA requirement stems from IEUA Urban Water Management Plan update.

12011.name: Broward County
12011.title: Established Data Center Market — Fort Lauderdale Metro
12011.description: Broward County hosts one of Florida's most active data center markets. CyrusOne operates a large campus in Pompano Beach; NTT Global Data Centers and T5 Data Centers have significant footprints in the county. Broward's position between Miami and Palm Beach, access to NAP of the Americas subsea cable terminus infrastructure, and Florida's exemption on data center equipment sales tax (§212.08(7)(fff) F.S.) make it a preferred location for financial services and enterprise disaster recovery workloads.
12011.notes: FL 2021 data center sales tax exemption significantly boosted new investment.

17197.name: Will County
17197.title: Hyperscale Data Center Campus — Joliet/Channahon Area
17197.description: Will County, southwest of Chicago, hosts Google's major data center campus near Channahon along the I-55 corridor. The campus, which began operations in 2016 and has undergone multiple expansions, is powered substantially by Illinois wind energy. The county participates in ComEd's economic development rate for large commercial and industrial customers, which benefits hyperscale data center operators. Illinois' data center tax incentive (35 ILCS 110/2d — Enterprise Zone Act exemption for qualified servers) also applies to eligible Will County facilities.
17197.notes: Google Channahon campus is one of the largest in the Midwest.

sources.label: Will County Center for Economic Development

20091.name: Johnson County
20091.title: Kansas City Metro Data Center Hub — Overland Park / Lenexa
20091.description: Johnson County anchors the Kansas City metropolitan data center market. Major facilities include Google Fiber's network hub, AT&T Central Office data centers in Overland Park, and several colocation operators serving Midwest enterprise clients. CenturyLink/Lumen Technologies maintains significant network infrastructure in the county. Kansas's data center incentive (K.S.A. 74-5065 — High Performance Incentive Program) provides investment tax credits for qualifying capital expenditures by operators meeting job-creation thresholds. Johnson County is consistently ranked among the top Midwest counties for business climate.
20091.notes: Kansas City metro straddles the KS/MO state line; Johnson County is the KS side.

22033.name: East Baton Rouge Parish
22033.title: Major Data Center Hub — Baton Rouge / Lumen Technologies Campus
22033.description: East Baton Rouge Parish hosts Lumen Technologies' (formerly CenturyLink) headquarters and one of the company's largest network data center campuses. Digital Realty and Cologix operate carrier-neutral colocation facilities in the parish. Louisiana's Digital Media and Software Tax Credit (R.S. 47:6022) and the Quality Jobs Program provide financial incentives for technology companies meeting payroll thresholds. Entergy Louisiana's competitive industrial power rates and the parish's central location on the Gulf Coast fiber corridor make it a preferred hub for regional financial services and energy sector IT workloads.
22033.notes: Louisiana parishes are equivalent to counties in other states.

23005.name: Cumberland County
23005.title: Data Center Siting Review — Portland Metro Energy Concerns
23005.description: Cumberland County, home to Portland, Maine's largest city, has seen growing data center interest driven by cold climate free-cooling advantages and proximity to Atlantic subsea cable landing sites. The Portland City Council adopted a resolution in 2024 directing the Planning Board to study large-scale data center siting impacts on the Unitil/CMP electric grid and local property tax base. Maine's renewable portfolio standard (35% by 2025, 80% by 2030) makes the region attractive for operators committed to clean-energy targets, but grid capacity constraints on CMP's transmission system have slowed permitting for large industrial electric loads.
23005.notes: Portland Planning Board study ongoing; no binding moratorium in effect.

sources.label: Portland ME — Planning and Urban Development

36059.name: Nassau County
36059.title: Long Island Data Center Corridor — Garden City / Syosset
36059.description: Nassau County anchors Long Island's data center market with facilities in Garden City, Syosset, and Bethpage. Equinix NY11 in Syosset, along with DataGryd and Lightpath carrier-neutral facilities, serve financial services and media workloads requiring low latency to New York City. The county benefits from PSEG Long Island's reliable power infrastructure and proximity to the trans-Atlantic cable system terminus at Brookhaven. New York State's Excelsior Jobs Program and the Empire State Digital Infrastructure Investment Program provide tax credits for qualifying capital projects.
36059.notes: Nassau proximity to NYC financial district drives latency-sensitive workloads.

sources.label: Empire State Development — Excelsior Jobs Program

37097.name: Iredell County
37097.title: Data Center Cooling Water Review — Lake Norman Watershed
37097.description: Iredell County, on the Lake Norman reservoir northwest of Charlotte, has attracted data center interest due to plentiful Duke Energy power access and available industrial land. The Iredell County Board of Commissioners adopted a Data Center Impact Study Ordinance in 2023 requiring applicants for facilities over 20 MW to submit a Water Withdrawal and Discharge Impact Assessment to protect Lake Norman water quality. DXC Technology and several smaller colocation operators have existing facilities in Statesville. Duke Energy's McGuire Nuclear Station on Lake Norman provides reliable baseload power that hyperscale operators view favorably.
37097.notes: Impact Study Ordinance applies to new data center construction permits.

39061.name: Hamilton County
39061.title: Major Data Center Investment — Cincinnati Metro
39061.description: Hamilton County anchors the Cincinnati data center market, which serves as a secondary hub to Columbus for Midwest cloud infrastructure. Amazon Web Services and Google maintain regional facilities in the Cincinnati metro. Cincinnati Bell Technology Solutions (now Zayo) operates carrier-neutral data centers with dense fiber connectivity. Ohio's Data Center Investment Tax Credit (ORC §5709.65) — 8% credit on capital expenditures above $100 million — applies to qualifying Hamilton County projects. Duke Energy Ohio provides competitive industrial rates under Schedule DCS.
39061.notes: Cincinnati is the financial services/healthcare IT hub for the Ohio River corridor.

sources.label: Ohio Revised Code §5709.65 — Data Center Tax Credit

42017.name: Bucks County
42017.title: Delaware Valley Data Center Corridor — Quakertown / Dublin
42017.description: Bucks County participates in the Philadelphia-area data center corridor that extends from Northern Virginia through New Jersey into southeastern Pennsylvania. NTT Global Data Centers and several wholesale colocation operators maintain facilities in Quakertown and the Route 309 technology corridor. PECO Energy's industrial electric rates and proximity to 800+ carrier-class fiber routes along the I-476 corridor make Bucks County an attractive overflow market for Northern Virginia-capacity-constrained operators. Pennsylvania's Keystone Opportunity Zones (KOZ) include portions of Quakertown with reduced business and property taxes for qualifying tenants.
42017.notes: Part of the Mid-Atlantic data center corridor from NOVA to Philadelphia.

sources.label: NTT Global Data Centers — Philadelphia Area

42091.name: Montgomery County
42091.title: Data Center Siting Overlay Study — Montgomery County
42091.description: Montgomery County, Pennsylvania's second most populous county, has seen proposals for large data center campuses in its western townships. The county Planning Commission initiated a Data Center Siting Overlay Study in 2023 after receiving applications for facilities totaling over 300 MW. Concerns include substation load impacts on the PECO/PPL transmission grid, emergency generator diesel fuel storage, and stormwater runoff from large impervious surfaces. The study recommends conditional zoning approval requiring power supply impact agreements with the local distribution utility.
42091.notes: Overlay study recommendations pending formal ordinance adoption.

49011.name: Davis County
49011.title: Northern Utah Data Center Campus — Novva / North Salt Lake
49011.description: Davis County, immediately north of Salt Lake County, hosts Novva Data Centers' flagship campus in North Salt Lake — a purpose-built hyperscale facility with 120+ MW of planned capacity. The campus benefits from Rocky Mountain Power's competitive industrial rates, abundant fiber access along the I-15 corridor, and Utah's arid climate enabling aggressive free-cooling designs. Utah's data center sales tax exemption (Utah Code §59-12-104(77)) applies to qualifying server and power equipment. Davis County's proximity to the NSA Utah Data Center in adjacent Salt Lake County has created a dense fiber ecosystem in the area.
49011.notes: Novva North Salt Lake campus is one of the largest purpose-built DC campuses in the Mountain West.

sources.label: Novva Data Centers — North Salt Lake Campus
Utah Tax Commission — Sales Tax Exemptions §59-12-104

51041.name: Chesterfield County
51041.title: AWS Data Center Cluster — Chesterfield / Richmond South
51041.description: Chesterfield County, south of Richmond, has emerged as an extension of Virginia's data center corridor with Amazon Web Services operating multiple facilities in the county's industrial parks. The county's Comprehensive Plan designates data center-compatible industrial zones along Route 288 and Midlothian Turnpike. Dominion Energy Virginia provides reliable three-phase industrial service with dedicated data center rate structures (Schedule TOU-DC). Virginia's Data Center Investment Grant and IT equipment sales tax exemption apply to qualifying Chesterfield projects meeting the $150 million investment threshold.
51041.notes: Chesterfield is one of the fastest-growing data center markets in the Greater Richmond area.

sources.label: Virginia VEDP — Data Center Investment Grant

53061.name: Snohomish County
53061.title: Microsoft Puget Sound AI Campus — Marysville / Everett Area
53061.description: Snohomish County hosts Microsoft's expanding Puget Sound data center campus in Marysville and adjacent communities north of Seattle. The campus represents a multi-billion-dollar investment in AI training and Azure cloud infrastructure, leveraging Puget Sound Energy's hydroelectric power mix and the region's naturally cool climate for free-air economization. Snohomish County PUD's industrial service territory provides highly reliable power with one of the highest hydroelectric generation percentages in the US. The county has streamlined permitting for data center projects under its Economic Development Strategic Plan.
